variables.content: Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

Fix the descreening parameter to $\gamma=1\,a_{0}^{-1}$ and consider the homogeneous electron gas (HEG) with density parameterized by $r_s$ via $n=(4\pi r_s^3/3)^{-1}$.  Define $\varepsilon_{C}^{\gamma=1}(n)$ as the correlation energy per particle for the HEG evaluated using the shifted-contour auxiliary-field Monte Carlo (SCAFMC) method implemented with a plane-wave finite supercell and without an explicit infinite-cell-size extrapolation.  The proposition is that the SCAFMC numerical values obtained at sampled $r_s=\{1,2,3,5,10,20\}$, subject to an estimated statistical uncertainty of about $10\%$ and to finite-cell-size effects that were not fully converged in this preliminary calculation, nevertheless provide an acceptably accurate estimate of the infinite-system $\varepsilon_{C}^{\gamma=1}(n)$ within the stated $\sim 10\%$ uncertainty for the specific purpose of parametrizing a practical local-density residual functional.
      [26,27]

Given finite-size effective-mass data points \\(\\{(N_{i},m^{*}(N_{i}))\\}\\) at several densities and considering the family of fits \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) parametrized by \\(\\gamma\\), the proposition is: adopting a fixed exponent value \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolations across the densities considered is justified because the resulting fits produce substantially better overall goodness-of-fit statistics as measured by the reduced \\(\\chi^{2}\\) values than alternative fixed exponents over the same data sets, and therefore using \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields reliable extrapolated values \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) for these DMC data.

For the homogeneous electron gas (HEG) with electron density specified by the Wigner–Seitz radius $r_{s}$ via $n=(4\pi r_{s}^{3}/3)^{-1}$ and for descreening parameter fixed to $\gamma=1\,a_{0}^{-1}$, the residual correlation energy per particle $\varepsilon_{C}^{\gamma=1}(n)$ was numerically estimated using the shifted-contour auxiliary-field Monte Carlo (SCAFMC) method implemented in a plane-wave finite supercell at sampled densities $r_{s}\in\{1,2,3,5,10,20\}$; these SCAFMC calculations were performed without a complete infinite-cell-size extrapolation and the Monte Carlo statistical uncertainty is estimated at roughly $10\%$.  Defining the ratio $\eta(r_{s})\equiv\varepsilon_{C}^{\gamma=1}(n)/\varepsilon_{C}^{\mathrm{LDA}}(n)$ to compare the SCAFMC residual correlation to the standard local-density-approximation (LDA) correlation per particle $\varepsilon_{C}^{\mathrm{LDA}}(n)$ for the same HEG density, the discrete SCAFMC estimates of $\eta(r_{s})$ were represented by the rational fit
      $\eta(r_{s})=\dfrac{A}{C_{0}+C_{1}r_{s}+r_{s}^{2}}$
      with fitted parameters $A=3.4602$, $C_{0}=3.2$, and $C_{1}=-0.9$.  Within the stated Monte Carlo uncertainty and the limitations of finite-cell calculations, the fitted $\eta(r_{s})$ yields $\eta(r_{s})<1$ for most densities relevant to confined systems (except at very high densities where the preliminary SCAFMC data indicate $\eta(r_{s})>1$), indicating that $\varepsilon_{C}^{\gamma=1}(n)$ is generally reduced relative to the standard LDA correlation in the density regime sampled.

Define a spatially varying effective local electron mass (in atomic units) as the pointwise functional
      $$
      m(\rho,J)(r)=\Big(\frac{4\pi\rho(r)}{3}\Big)^{1/3}\,r_s(r),
      $$
      where $r_s(r)$ is obtained by inverting the fitted HEG relation $r_s=J_1^{-1}(J/\rho)$; using this definition of $m(\rho,J)$ in the local correlation-energy integrand enforces the correct homogeneous-coordinate-scaling behavior for the local HEG mapping and effectively adjusts the local kinetic-energy scale to improve agreement between the local-inhomogeneous approximation and HEG-derived correlation formulas.

For the transverse-field XY spin chain defined above with anisotropy $\\eta=0.5$ and decay exponent $\\alpha=2$, the finite-size extrapolation of pseudocritical fields $\\gamma_c^{*}(N)$ (defined as the peak positions of $\\chi_F$ for each finite $N$) is performed by plotting $\\gamma_c^{*}(N)$ versus $1/N^{1/\\nu}$ and extrapolating to $N\\to\\infty$. In this procedure the correlation-length exponent is fixed a priori to the two-dimensional classical Ising value
      $$
      \\nu = 1,
      $$
      and this fixed value $\\nu=1$ is adopted as an input (not fitted) in the linear extrapolation versus $1/N^{1/\\nu}$ used to obtain the thermodynamic critical field $\\gamma_c$ from finite-$N$ pseudocritical points.

The claim: "Finite-size-scaling (FSS) measurements of $\partial\log P_\infty/\partial\gamma$ and of the finite-cluster mass $S$ at the apparent critical point $\gamma_c$ yield scaling exponents that approach the 2D percolation expectations ($\nu=4/3$, $\hat{\gamma}=43/18$) for decay exponents $\alpha\gtrsim4$, i.e. finite-size and crossover corrections are small enough in the studied $L$-range that the asymptotic universality class can be identified" means the following. For square-lattice-based networks with long-range links drawn from P(r) ∝ r^{-α}, the peak scaling of ∂ log P_∞/∂γ at γ_c is fitted to a power law in linear system size L as L^{1/ν} and the finite-cluster mass S at γ_c is fitted as S_L(γ_c) ∼ L^{Ĝ/ν}; the proposition is that the observed approach of the fitted exponents to the 2D percolation values ν=4/3 and Ĝ=43/18 for α ≳ 4 reflects genuine asymptotic two-dimensional universality rather than being an artifact of the finite L range or of slowly decaying crossover corrections from effective higher-dimensional behaviour.
